questions.question: Define a node structure for a singly linked list in C. Write functions to create a list, insert a node at the beginning, insert at the end, and display the list.
keyPoints: Define struct node with data and next pointer
Insert at beginning: create node, point to current head, update head
Insert at end: traverse to last node, link new node, set new node next to NULL
Display: traverse from head, print each node data, stop at NULL
Handle edge case of empty list for all operations

questions.question: Write a C function to delete a node with a given key from a singly linked list. Handle all edge cases including deleting the head node and deleting from an empty list.
keyPoints: Check if list is empty (head == NULL)
Special case: if head node contains the key, update head and free old head
General case: traverse with two pointers (prev and current)
When found: set prev->next = current->next, then free(current)
If key not found after full traversal, report element not present

questions.question: Explain the concept of self-referential structures in C. How are they used to represent a chain? Illustrate with a diagram.
keyPoints: Self-referential structure: a struct containing a pointer to its own type
Node definition: struct node { int data; struct node *next; };
The next pointer creates the link between consecutive nodes
Draw diagram showing nodes with data fields and arrows representing next pointers
Show head pointer and NULL termination

questions.question: Write a C function to count the number of nodes in a singly linked list. Provide both iterative and recursive versions.
keyPoints: Iterative: initialize count=0, traverse with while(current != NULL), increment count
Recursive: base case returns 0 when node is NULL, recursive case returns 1 + count(node->next)
Both return the same result; iterative uses O(1) space, recursive uses O(n) stack space
Time complexity is O(n) for both approaches

questions.question: Compare the representation of a list using arrays versus chains in C. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages with respect to memory, insertion, deletion, and access time.
keyPoints: Array: contiguous memory, O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete due to shifting
Chain: non-contiguous memory, O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete at known position
Array: fixed size (static) or resize overhead (dynamic), good cache locality
Chain: grows one node at a time with no wasted space, but each node has pointer overhead
Choice depends on operation frequency: frequent access favors arrays, frequent insert/delete favors chains
